quote: Just the I mean, my winter power bills are, all the way up into the 5 hundreds.
stewart_read: Liz hands the rep a concrete bill-pain anchor in the first 45 seconds — winter bills in the $500s. The rep responds with 'Oh, jeez.' and then immediately moves to address confirmation. The bill is captured but never flipped. Per the schema, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the move is to flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The rep had the number and walked past the leverage.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep was deferring the flip to after full qualification was complete. Rejected — the rep never returns to the bill figure at any point in the call, and the call ends without a booking, so no later flip was ever attempted.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the schema's capture-then-flip pattern. The suggested direction here is something like: 'Five hundred dollars every winter — that's the bill solar is designed to replace. Let me make sure we can size this right for you.' Manager should flag this as Stage 3's suggested direction, not Ion canonical language, and calibrate with Kenny's voice before using it as a floor exemplar.

quote: Oh, what I'm surprised you didn't get this information. I've provided all of this information.
stewart_read: This is a process-competence failure signal, not a legitimacy-anxiety moment. Liz expected her prior form data to be on file. The rep's response ('Sometimes, when it gets pulled up, it's not always the right one. I don't know why. It just swaps it with the utility') is a vague deflection that neither resolves the concern nor acknowledges the gap. The correct move is honest specificity: acknowledge the data gap, take ownership briefly, and move forward without dwelling. The deflection leaves the process failure unaddressed and slightly erodes the credibility the intro established.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been trying to minimize friction by not dwelling on the CRM gap. Rejected — the deflection ('I don't know why') signals uncertainty rather than confidence, which compounds rather than resolves the concern.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on owning process gaps briefly and moving forward: 'You're right, I should have had that — let me just confirm a couple of things so we're working from the same page.' One sentence of ownership, then redirect. Dwelling or deflecting both cost more than a clean acknowledgment.

stewart_read: Liz volunteers a specific product request — battery backup — unprompted. This is a high-value enthusiasm signal: she knows what she wants, she has an existing system, and she is describing a specific gap (undersized system, one broken panel, no storage). The rep acknowledges it ('K. Battery backup. Let me get that down.') and confirms the add-on scope correctly. The procedural handling is adequate. The coaching gap is tonal: the rep treats this as a checkbox item rather than a moment of genuine alignment. A warmer acknowledgment — 'Battery backup is exactly what the Powerwall is built for, and it pairs well with an add-on' — would have elevated the customer's confidence that she called the right company. The rep's flat acknowledgment is a missed warmth moment, not a substantive product failure.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep correctly avoided naming the Powerwall specifically, which is closer-tier product content per schema coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline. This is procedurally correct. The coaching gap is tonal, not substantive.
coaching_implication: The rep held setter scope correctly by not going into product specs. Coach on warmth in the acknowledgment — the customer just told you exactly what she wants. A brief 'That's something we can absolutely do' before moving to the next question costs nothing and builds confidence. This is an energy-match gap, not a scope-creep gap.
schema_reference: cross_sell_signals — NOTE: battery_storage_inquiry is explicitly listed as TBD in the schema (cross_sell_signals section); this reference is Stage 3's analytical inference, not a populated schema entry.

quote: I've this property was previously owned by a guy that owns an electric company. And so Oh, okay. I'm assuming that things just fell off the truck and landed on his house.
stewart_read: The rep enters orphaned territory (existing solar on property) but does not complete the schema-defined orphaned_flow checklist. Specifically: (1) own-vs-lease was never asked — the rep confirmed it wasn't Pink Solar but did not establish ownership status of the existing system; (2) system operational status was never explicitly confirmed — Liz mentioned one broken panel but the rep never asked 'Is the system currently working?' as the checklist requires. The rep's Pink Solar check is correct procedure, but it is the only orphaned_flow item completed. The ownership and operational-status gaps are schema-defined hard-DQ triggers if the answers are wrong.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have inferred ownership from context (Liz said 'I do have existing solar on the property' in a possessive framing). Rejected as sufficient — the schema requires explicit confirmation, and the prior-owner context Liz provided ('previously owned by a guy') makes the ownership question more important, not less.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the orphaned_flow checklist. Two items were skipped that carry hard-DQ implications: own-vs-lease and system operational status. On a call where the customer has a non-standard existing system (20 panels, one broken, prior-owner install), these are not optional checkboxes.

quote: No. That's a metal roof over a garage.
stewart_read: Liz's answer contains two distinct signals the rep does not unpack: (1) the roof is metal, which affects panel mounting and may affect installer compatibility; (2) the existing panels are on a garage, not the main house — relevant to add-on sizing, shading analysis, and potentially whether the main house roof is a separate opportunity. The rep writes it down and moves on. Neither the metal-roof implications nor the garage-vs-main-house distinction is explored. The rep also never asks the roof's age, which is a required verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been deferring roof-detail questions to the closer/design team as setter-scope discipline. Partially valid — but roof age is a required verify item, not closer-tier content, and the garage distinction is a qualification-relevant fact, not a technical detail.
coaching_implication: Two coaching points here: (1) Roof age is a required verify item — the rep skipped it entirely. (2) The garage-vs-main-house distinction should have prompted a follow-up: 'Is the existing system on the garage or the main house?' This affects how the design team sizes the add-on and is a qualification-relevant fact the setter should capture.

quote: Apparently, we're not able to work with WASCO, because as of right now, their the utility company is not accepting any solar applications and does not have any plans to reopen their solar program at the at the moment.
stewart_read: The rep discovers WASCO's net metering freeze after spending approximately 90 seconds walking Liz toward bill collection. Liz already knew about the freeze ('No. They're not accepting that metering. I know that. Yeah.'). The rep's information was 'updated as of a year ago' — meaning this was checkable before the call. The knowledge gap here is a pre-call preparation failure: utility eligibility for the customer's specific co-op should have been verified before dialing, or at minimum before initiating bill collection. The rep's handling of the discovery is honest ('I don't wanna be wasting your time') but the exit is structurally fragile.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may not have had access to utility eligibility data before the call, making this a systems gap rather than a rep preparation gap. Possible — but the rep's own statement ('this looks like it was updated as of a year ago') implies the information was accessible in the rep's tools during the call, which means it was accessible before the call.
coaching_implication: Two-part coaching: (1) Pre-call prep discipline — utility eligibility for non-standard co-ops (WASCO, rural electric co-ops) should be checked before dialing, not discovered mid-bill-collection. (2) The callback exit needs structure — see coaching_focus priority 2.
schema_reference: bill_collection — NOTE: the schema's bill_collection section does not specify that utility eligibility must be confirmed before bill collection begins. The sequencing argument here is Stage 3's analytical inference: checking utility eligibility before investing time in bill collection is a logical efficiency discipline, not a schema-mandated sequence.

quote: Well, yeah, the last thing we'll need in order for my company to be able to size out the additional panels as well as the battery as well, I just wanna take a look at the most recent electrical electric bill.
stewart_read: By the time the rep moves to bill collection at 03:16, the verify section is materially incomplete. Items confirmed: address, homeowner status, sole title holder, utility company, average bill (verbal), roof type (partial). Items skipped: prior_designs (has she seen a solar design for this home?), residence_length, credit qualifier. The prior_designs item is particularly relevant here — Liz has an existing system and a specific add-on need, making competitive context more important than on a cold lead. The credit qualifier was never asked. Per schema verify, required_completion_threshold is 6 of 8 items; the rep is at approximately 5 of 8 before the call ends without a booking.
alternative_hypothesis: The call ended before the rep could complete verify, so some skipped items may have been planned for later. Rejected — the rep moved to bill collection before completing verify, and the call ended at the bill-collection stage. The skipped items were not deferred; they were missed.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the verify checklist against this transcript. Three items were skipped before bill collection was initiated. Prior_designs is the highest-value miss on this specific call — a customer with an existing system and a specific add-on need almost certainly has competitive context worth surfacing.

quote: So I'll I'll I'll message them right now, and then I'll give you a callback with an update later. Does that work for you?
stewart_read: The rep exits with a callback promise that has no locked time, no specific scope ('I'll check with my design team'), and no confirmation of what Liz should expect. Per schema analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time has materially higher no-show risk than a scheduled appointment. The bill refusal at 05:10 ('I don't wanna have you grab all that for me') is a defensible call given genuine WASCO uncertainty — collecting a bill for a potentially ineligible utility is a reasonable thing to defer. The primary coaching point at this moment is the unstructured callback, not the bill refusal.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged that locking a callback time was premature given genuine uncertainty about WASCO eligibility. Partially valid — but a locked callback time ('I'll call you back by end of day tomorrow') costs nothing and materially improves show rate regardless of what the design team confirms.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on the locked-callback discipline: even when the outcome is uncertain, the next step should be specific. Suggested direction: 'Let me check with the design team today — can I call you back tomorrow at [specific time]?' This converts a soft callback into a scheduled touchpoint and protects the lead.
